channel.description: Technology. Code. Living relentlessly in the real world.

item.title: Film Fight 2021: April
item.description:
For April’s Film Fight, we have 9 films.

Concrete Cowboy

#FF21 Concrete Cowboy makes a decent job of well-worn material (troubled teen sent to live in fish out of water place).

There are some threads that are very underdeveloped that should've been excised, but the main thrust is fine.

An okay film.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 4, 2021

Chaos Walking

#FF21 Chaos Walking is a bit of a mess. There really isn't any character development, the action is boring, and the main theme doesn't work particularly well.

There are moments where it looks like they'll do something interesting with the Noise conceit, but nope.

Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 4, 2021

News of the World

#FF21 News Of The World is... fine. Hanks is as reliable as always, and the Helena Zengel puts in an excellent performance.

It looks decent enough, it's got some moments, but is entirely predictable. There's absolutely nothing new being offered here.

Okay.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 11, 2021

The Map Of Tiny Perfect Things

#FF21 The Map Of Tiny Perfect Things is a fun teen romance about two kids caught in a time loop in their small town.

The leads are charming, the loops work well, and it all builds up to a schmaltzy, predictable, but pleasant ending.

Solid.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 11, 2021

Run

#FF21 Run is an okay, if silly and cliched horror.

It's got moments of tension, despite the predictability, and both leads turn in good performances.

Nothing special but not bad. Pretty average.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 18, 2021

Promising Young Woman

#FF21 Promising Young Woman is excellent; snapping between revenge horror and sweet romance.

The music and visuals are great together; sharp, saturated, tightly edited.

But it's the lead performance that sells it.

The very end is too neat, but it doesn't matter. Must see.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 18, 2021

Nobody

#FF21 Nobody is an okay, cut-rate John Wick knock-off.

Odenkirk is good, but doesn't get enough to work with. Most of the action isn't on-par with what it's trying to copy, but the pacing keeps thinks moving forward anyway.

Fine but forgettable.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 18, 2021

Proxima

#FF21 Proxima is a slow, deliberate take on motherhood, from the perspective of an astronaut preparing for a year away.

Eva Green handles the mixture of physical and emotional challenge with the evenness expected of her exceptionally well.

A little thin in places, but worth it.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 18, 2021

Thunder Force

#FF21 Thunder Force is dull almost from start to finish, which ain't great for a comedy.

It wastes Octavia Spencer throughout, with McCarthy going through twice reheated schtick. Scenes with Bateman as a crab-man are the only ones with laughs.

Avoid.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 18, 2021

The April Winner

An easy one: Promising Young Woman has the story and style to take it here, and a better than average shot at film of the year.

item.title: Film Fight 2021: March
item.description:
For March’s Film Fight we have 9 films.

Coming 2 America

#FF21 Coming 2 America is... fine. It does nothing new or interesting, spending too long falling into the revisiting all of the same characters rather than establish much that is new.

The dated elements are, thankfully, updated here.

A few nice moments, but nothing spectacular.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 7, 2021

Moxie

#FF21 Moxie is a nice mix of rebelling, getting things a bit wrong, and trying harder. There are moments that are a bit earnest and obvious, and others that hit pretty hard.

It's wrapped in a great soundtrack, is directed with a solid pace, and sets a decent tone.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 21, 2021

Barb & Star Go To Vista Del Mar

#FF21 Barb & Star Go To Vista Del Mar is surprisingly excellent.

It's tone veers from a little odd to very weird; it reminds me a lot of Anchorman, in the best way.

The cast are excellent, the plot is fine but doesn't matter, and I laughed a lot.

Watch this film.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 21, 2021

Kajillionaire

#FF21 Kajillionaire is an offbeat film about a grifter family, and what they put their daughter through.

Evan Rachel Woods is like you've never seen here before, nailing the socially awkward and downtrodden lead who begins to glimpse a better life.

A little slow, but solid.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 21, 2021

Dead Pigs

#FF21 Dead Pigs appears at first to be a series of vignettes about commercialism in China, but it's not long before the pieces start to interlock.

The direction here is incredible: clear, dynamic, beautifully light, frequently picking slightly longer shots.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 21, 2021

Judas And The Black Messiah

#FF21 Judas And The Black Messiah tells the story of Fred Hampton and Bill O'Neal incredibly.

The lead performances are great; empathetic and tragic at the same time. Looks great too; the opening section had me grinning with some subtle choices.

A must see.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 21, 2021

Fire Will Come

#FF21 Fire Will Come is the story of a released arsonist in rural Spain.

It's beautiful, in colour and composition, but also incredibly slow. We don't see his character develop or learn much about him.

I'm fine with an ambiguous ending, but make me care before we get there. Meh

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 28, 2021

Bad Trip

#FF21 Bad Trip takes the prank-reality-meets-story concept from Bad Grandpa and applies it to a road trip/romantic comedy.

It's not amazing. There are funny moments, but the reliance on shock and gross-out humour pulls this film down. It's all been done before.

Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 28, 2021

Bacurau

#FF21 Bacurau is about colonialism and culture, through the lens of The Most Dangerous Game.

Strange, dark, bloody, funny; it shows people trying to live their very different lives, and the encroaching outsiders. Some stunning imagery.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) April 4, 2021

The March Winner

A genuinely difficult month to pick. While Barb & Star is the best comedy I’ve seen in ages, the real contenders are Judas & The Black Messiah and Dead Pigs. I think Judas & The Black Messiah edges it simply because if I had to pick one to watch again soon, it’d be that.

item.title: Film Fight 2021: February
item.description:
Eight films for February’s Film Fight…

Possessor

#FF21 Possessor is an uncomfortable by fantastic film about an assassin who takes over people's bodies.

Body horror is at the core, as the lead starts to lose her mind. It'll make your skin crawl in places, with some sharp twists and deep discomfort.

An excellent watch.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) February 7, 2021

Bliss

#FF21 Bliss sees Owen Wilson and Salma Hayek confront the reality of their world, believing it to be a simulation.

Is it? Isn't it? You won't care at all by the end. Poor performances and clanky philosophising don't help an already dull script.

Avoid.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) February 7, 2021

Greenland

#FF21 Greenland is better than most low-mid budget, VOD fodder. That's not to say it's great.

The middle chunk ("separation") is turgid and predictable, for example.

That said, it's a decent disaster flick: easy, entertaining, silly. Watchable.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) February 15, 2021

Malcolm & Marie

#FF21 Malcolm & Marie is what I like in a good performance piece: two very skilled actors chewing the scenery, as characters who you both love and hate.

The stagey monologues are mostly gripping; the presentation clear & crisp; and the flow works well.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) February 15, 2021

The White Tiger

#FF21 The White Tiger sees the rise of a poor man in India to be the driver of a rich family and beyond; showing the abuses inherent in a class system.

The pacing is a little off towards the end, but for the most part the film is darkly funny, a little heartbreaking, and decent.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 6, 2021

Portrait Of A Lady On Fire

#FF21 Portrait Of A Lady On Fire was a little disappointing, but not bad, largely due to hype.

It's a gorgeous film throughout, in framing and colour choices. The final scene has an incredible performance, that outshines the rest of the movie.

Slow, a little dull, not for me.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 6, 2021

I Care A Lot

#FF21 I Care A Lot sees Rosamund Pike but in an incredible performance as the uncaring guardian of vulnerable elderly people she exploits.

While the film is entertaining, I think we're supposed to eventually get on side with her as she stands up to a mob boss. It fails.

Good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 6, 2021

Escape From Pretoria

#FF21 Escape From Pretoria is a surprisingly entertaining story about activists attempting a prison escape during apartheid.

It does a great job of pacing, tension, and spatial awareness. You're rarely in doubt as to what is happening and why.

Very watchable.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) March 6, 2021

The February Winner

A tough call. Malcolm & Marie would take it most months, but I think the winner is Possessor. Some of those images will stay with me for a very long time.

item.title: Film Fight 2021: January
item.description:
A fairly good start to the year despite, or maybe because of, the lack of cinemas. I’m keeping the change I made during lockdown: rather than fairly strictly new releases from the last month or so, it’s anything since all of this started since timeliness is less relevant. I’ll start disallowing the older films in that at some point.

I watched 11 new films in January.

Saint Frances

#FF21 Saint Frances is a film dealing with issues that we don't talk about enough, while deftly not making a big deal about them unless it matters.

It's at times funny, heartbreaking, sombre and joyous.

I don't think there was a single scene that didn't work.

Very good!

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 4, 2021

Babyteeth

#FF21 Babyteeth is an incredible yet difficult film about a dying teen and her longing for a relationship with a man whose life is in ruins; captured in moments that are both sad and relatable.

It explores different kinds of vulnerability perfectly.

An excellent but uneasy film

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 4, 2021

Rogue City

#FF21 Rogue City spends its first half as a dull, overwrought action film about corrupt cops and double crosses.

It gets better towards the end, with some decent sequences, but not enough to wade through that terrible first half.

Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 10, 2021

Pieces Of A Woman

#FF21 Pieces Of A Woman opens with a stunning, devastating, tearjerking, masterclass of a one-shot, showing a home birth.

It falters after that. Great performances start to serve an uninteresting family drama, rather than the intimate portrait this should have been.

Has moments

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 10, 2021

Superintelligence

#FF21 Superintelligence is an entirely forgettable, and laugh-free, comedy about an AI who needs to be convinced to not kill humanity.

The cast are solid, but given absolutely nothing to work with; with several plot points that seem at odds with everything else.

Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 10, 2021

Outside The Wire

#FF21 Outside The Wire is a pretty middling action film. The two leads turn in decent performances, but the plot is muddled and the action badly shot; far too cut up to be exciting.

The anti-war message is interesting, but heavy handed.

Forgettable but fine.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 17, 2021

Soul

#FF21 Soul is as incredibly designed and animated as you expect from a top-tier Pixar movie.

The cast, the music, the mood; all great. A fairly minor criticism would be that it feels like the Pixar formula: fantastically executed, great message, but nothing new.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 17, 2021

Ham On Rye

#FF21 Ham On Rye matches the loose feeling, end-of-school indie (Dazed And Confused) with something quite different.

Playing expectations and anxieties around the importance of these days vs the reality, we see a second half that deftly turns a little dark.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 24, 2021

Arkansas

#FF21 Arkansas wraps its plots up a little too neatly, but is a surprisingly enjoyable, slice-of-life tale of two drug dealers, their mentors, and more.

The tone is a little tongue-in-cheek, some charm, with a more brutal streak mixed in.

Worth your time.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 24, 2021

One Night In Miami

#FF21 One Night In Miami is a good feature debut: looking at a shared night between Mohammed Ali, Malcolm X, Sam Cooke, and Jim Brown.

It's stage origins are clear: this is performance-forward, and location-limited. This mostly works, but at times the energy dips.

Worth seeing.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 31, 2021

Synchronic

#FF21 Synchronic sees Benson/Moorhead work through the daily drudge of life as a paramedic, brotherly friendship, and living with your choices; with a strange twist.

A little dreary, but that's kinda the point. The two leads are solid.

Go in as cold as you can, to enjoy it most

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 31, 2021

The January Winner

There’s some really good options for January, but I think I’m going to go with Babyteeth.

item.title: Film Fight 2020: Finale
item.description:
Film Fight, for the first time in years, ran roughly to schedule. Most of the monthly posts went up on time! Hurrah!

The downside is that the entire film release business was pretty hampered by the ongoing Covid-19 pandemic, with cinemas being shut for most of the year. Given this meant my attention was elsewhere and timeliness was less of an issue, I made a change to the rules: rather than only reviewing films I saw within roughly a month of their release, I’d review anything that came out in 2020, whenever I finally saw it.

As we go into 2021, I’ll start ageing out films from eligibility in some way or another, but it’s not like any of this particularly matters. The good films will still be good.

As per the caveat every year, Film Fight is done in a (non particularly serious) knock-out style: it can pick my favourite film of the year, but nothing else. (Also the very idea of a single favourite film is a bit silly.)

First up, the honourable mentions: films that were good but didn’t win their month:

  • Jojo Rabbit
  • Little Women
  • The Lighthouse
  • The Personal History of David Copperfield
  • Horse Girl
  • Freaks
  • The Platform
  • Vivarium
  • Lost Girls
  • Sweetheart
  • Extraction
  • Swallow
  • Sea Fever
  • The Lovebirds
  • Ultras
  • Da 5 Bloods
  • The Banker
  • Family Romance, LLC
  • Palm Springs
  • Nobody Knows I’m Here
  • 7500
  • Days of the Bagnold Summer
  • Tenet
  • The King of Staten Island
  • Greyhound
  • Mank

All of those are worth seeing, but worth pointing out Palm Springs and Tenet as the only two films from this year I’ve seen twice; and I’d happily watch either again immediately.

Now, the monthly winners:

  • January: Parasite
  • February: Uncut Gems
  • March: The Invisible Man
  • April: System Crasher
  • May: Uncorked
  • June: The Orphanage
  • July: The Assistant
  • August: Never Rarely Sometimes Always
  • September: Get Duked
  • October: The Trial of the Chicago 7
  • November: His House
  • December: Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom

As always, the year was front heavy, but wasn’t followed up by many Summer blockbusters or late-year prestige dramas.

I remember how difficult System Crasher was to watch, but it’s incredibly powerful too. A system failing a young girl with serious issues. Seek it out.

Likewise, The Assistant is important but much quieter, showing how quietly and complacently people become part of a system that exists to harm others, backed up by an excellent lead.

Uncut Gems is anxiety-ridden, tense, aggrevating, and absolutely gripping. It’s great to see Sandler back in a decent role, and the rest of the cast back him up. A stressful watch.

Finally, to a winner: it feels like a long time ago that I saw it, and definitely not like within the last year. I had wanted to rewatch Parasite before making a decision here but, honestly, it’s not much of a contest. There have been some incredible films over the last 12 months, despite the obvious challenges, but Parasite gets everything right. It’s beautiful, well-paced, surprising, and with a lot of depth. It seems like that rare film that almost everyone will get something from.

item.title: Film Fight 2020: December
item.description:
A decent number of films for December with 10 new films, but are they any good?

Mank

#FF20 Mank is an ode to classic cinema. It takes on the look, movement, audio etc, down to lens choices.

The cast are excellent, with Oldman and Seyfried the stand-outs.

The back-and-forth structure doesn't work for me, but I understand the why.

A solid movie.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) December 6, 2020

Inheritance

#FF20 Inheritance is not a good movie.

At every turn, the characters make bizarre choices, driven by handwaved issues, that simply do not make sense.

The performances are so-so, with British actors doing American accents that fall apart pretty consistently.

Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) December 6, 2020

Ava

#FF20 Ava is a fairly forgettable film about an assassin who starts asking too many questions. A solid main cast doesn't help a cliched plot, especially with a dull family b-plot in the background.

A few okay action moments, but you can safely skip this.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) December 13, 2020

Archenemy

#FF20 Archenemy fails to deliver much beyond some mildly interesting visuals, a contrast between neon and grimy grain.

The story is predictable, and the dialogue dull. There's little to care about here, and a lot to dislike.

Avoid.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) December 20, 2020

Yes, God, Yes

#FF20 Yes, God, Yes is a fairly limp light comedy about a Christian high school retreat, and some of the hypocrisy around sex.

It doesn't have enough teeth to deliver a message, or enough laughs to be particularly entertaining.

Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) December 20, 2020

Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom

#FF20 Ma Rainey's Black Bottom is a fantastic performance showcase for both Viola Davis and Chadwick Boseman; both very different, but captivating.

Some of the longer monologues feel a little out of place, but are well executed.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) December 27, 2020

The Midnight Sky

#FF20 The Midnight Sky gets more disappointing the longer it goes on. It begins beautifully, and slowly.

What should have been a quiet, contemplative movie about what we've done to the world and second chances, gives way to cliche and unnecessary contrivance.

Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) December 27, 2020

Wonder Woman 1984

#FF20 Wonder Woman 1984 squanders a good cast on a mess of a film.

Overlong, dull, dumb, and badly paced. It's a real slog.

Even the action is uninspired, badly edited, and visually poor. Suprising, given the original.

There’s little to recommend here. Avoid.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 3, 2021

Greyhound

#FF20 Greyhound is an unusual film.

It doesn't have much of a defined structure: focussing on the constant but quiet action of this kind of naval combat.

It's quite human, despite most of the people being abstract figures on distant ships.

Grounded, steady, solid. Watchable.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 3, 2021

The 2nd

#FF20 The 2nd is one of the worst films I saw in 2020.

Nonsensical plot, bad action, bad acting; the works. None of it makes any sense, and it's not even particularly entertaining along the way.

Big avoid here.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) January 3, 2021

The December Winner

There was a surprising amount of bad stuff this month, but Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom is a more than deserving winner, for those excellent lead performances.

item.title: Film Fight 2020: November
item.description:
For November’s Film Fight, we have 6 films.

Borat Subsequent Moviefilm

#FF20 Borat Subsequent Moviefilm is a bit of a tired take on Sascha Baron-Cohen's spoof characters. There's little here you haven't seen before, with empty shocks and janky setups; all merged together to form a "plot".

The laughs are few. This is a dull, dated film. Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) November 15, 2020

His House

#FF20 His House is an excellent horror film, that blends the supernatural with the psychological threats of every day life for refugees. It plays with our expectations, while putting forward messages about integration.

An exceptional debut, great performances, and a solid horror

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) November 15, 2020

#Alive

#FF20 #Alive is an okay South Korean zombie film.

The characters and set-up are fine, but there's little new here. The social media framing doesn't go far enough to give a new spin.

A fine 90 minutes, but not groundbreaking.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) November 15, 2020

The King of Staten Island

#FF20 The King of Staten Island is a better comedy than I expected. Both Pete Davidson and Bill Burr deliver some great moments, grounded in some emotional weight.

Good pace, good length, but with one or two dead-ends.

Pretty good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) November 15, 2020

The Babysitter: Killer Queen

#FF20 The Babysitter: Killer Queen is very much a sequel to the first one, amped up.

Overstylised, ridiculous gore, over-the-top acting, and more silliness. The story is pretty flimsy, with a few set-ups for not very much pay-off.

Fine if you want something easy and dumb.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) November 15, 2020

Hillbilly Elegy

#FF20 Hillbilly Elegy fails to deliver a compelling narrative.

There’s the heart of a good story here, brought down by Hollywood schmaltz and cliched plot devices.

Glenn Close and Amy Adam’s put in fantastic performances, but Basso less so.

Good and bad parts. Flawed.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) November 29, 2020

The November Winner

The winner is His House, a must-see horror film.

item.title: Film Fight 2020: October
item.description:
My concentration was elsewhere so only three films in October.

Enola Holmes

#FF20 Enola Holmes didn't work for me. While the cast are charming, the plot they're running through feels forced and overlong; a series of set pieces and encounters, flimsily connected. None of the characters earned their emotional beats.

Honestly, a bit dull.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) October 11, 2020

The Other Lamb

#FF20 The Other Lamb follows a cult member starting to become disillusioned with her leader.

This film is gorgeous: from the saturated colours against the natural, to some of the vistas. Stunning cinematogrpahy.

The plot is lacking. The changes and ending feel cliched. Decent.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) October 26, 2020

The Trial of The Chicago 7

#FF20 The Trial Of The Chicago 7 is a look at government power, protest, and counter-culture, through the lens of a courtroom drama.

Strong performances from an excellent cast, tight dialogue, and excellent structuring have the film going at a great pace.

Very good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) October 31, 2020

The October Winner

The winner for October is The Trial Of The Chicago 7, a timely reminder of systemic injustice.

item.title: Film Fight 2020: September
item.description:
A reasonable month, with 6 new films…

Get Duked

#FF20 Get Duked is a decent dark comedy, that clearly takes inspiration from Hot Fuzz, both tonally and visually.

It's not quite as slick as Wright's work, but has some very funny moments, neat visuals, and a fun plot.

A solid feature debut. Good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) September 6, 2020

I’m Thinking of Ending Things

#FF20 I'm Thinking of Ending Things is a difficult movie.

The conversation at the start is excellent, and builds tension, warps into unease, and a creeping horror. The last act drops the ball, focussing on indulgence, not delivery.

Performances are all excellent.

Very mixed.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) September 6, 2020

Spree

#FF20 Spree is a fairly broad and somewhat satirical look at what streaming and influencer culture does to people, as we follow a guy who decides on a dangerous path forward.

Reasonably well shot, decent pacing, and a bit silly. It's all fairly predictable. Okay.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) September 27, 2020

The Devil All The Time

#FF20 The Devil All The Time is less than the sum of its parts.

A really good cast are mired in various plots we've seen before that tie together in a way that seems forced. It suffers from horrible narration: have the actors show me, don't tell me.

Pretty forgettable. Skip.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) September 27, 2020

Freaks: You’re One Of Us

#FF20 Freaks: You're One Of Us is a German superhero story about people whose powers have been suppressed.

It's fine, if entirely predictable and chock full of stereotypes. There are a few neat moments, but mostly this is just mild entertainment.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) October 3, 2020

Bill & Ted Face The Music

#FF20 Bill & Ted Face The Music is everything no-one needed. It brings back old characters with nods and winks, adds new ones that are uninteresting, and mostly tries to get by on nostalgia.

Twenty minutes in you'll know exactly what's going to happen.

Tired and dull.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) October 3, 2020

The September Winner

Turns out there wasn’t much worthwhile this month, so the winner is Get Duked.

item.title: Film Fight 2020: August
item.description:
A little quieter than recent months, but still 7 new films.

7500

#FF20 7500 says Joseph Gordon-Levitt as a co-pilot fighting off a hijacking attempt.

A solid one location film, with great sound design, tense moments, and some neat plotting. The last 20-30 minutes drag a little, but it holds up overall.

Worth seeing.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) August 9, 2020

The Vast of Night

#FF20 The Vast of Night never quite reaches where it should.

The performances are fine with some very neat shots (and one that is a bit too clever), but the script mistakes running frantically between places with action. Much of the story is flat and expositional.

Meh.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) August 9, 2020

Project Power

#FF20 Project Power is a bit of a mess.

Despite a solid cast, it's a bit of a mess. The script feels underwritten, bumbling from set piece to set piece in a clunky way.

The action itself is bad: a mess of edits that makes it difficult to enjoy any of it.

Really not good.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) August 16, 2020

Days of the Bagnold Summer

#FF20 Days of the Bagnold Summer is a charming story about a single mum and her metalhead son.

It perfectly plays teenage grump and awkwardness against a librarian in cardigan. The neat cinematography provides lots of beautiful shots and framing.

Funny, sweet, and worth seeing.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) August 22, 2020

An American Pickle

#FF20 An American Pickle is a fish out of water buddy comedy, about a man preserved in brine for 100 years.

It has moments of humour, but is ultimately a bit one note. Neither of Seth Rogan's characters are particularly 2-dimensional.

Meh.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) August 22, 2020

Never Rarely Sometimes Always

#FF20 Never Rarely Sometimes Always is an important look at how difficult it can be to get an abortion in the US.

Shot in a grounded style, the quietness amplifies the reality being shown. The performances are equally subtle.

Sometimes difficult viewing, but worth seeing.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) August 31, 2020

Tenet

#FF29 Tenet is a Nolan film, through and through.

Spectacular action and a complicated narrative, with a great performance by Washington. Also suffers from being excessively expositional, and having terrible sound design, as with most of his films.

Worth seeing at least once.

— Gary Fleming (@garyfleming) August 31, 2020

The August Winner

The winner for August is Never Rarely Sometimes Always, a difficult but important watch, that’s well put together.
